S&P 500 EPS & Valuation Terminal v2

to

What this is

The S&P 500 is a basket of roughly 500 of the largest publicly traded companies in the United States, and its price level is simply the product of two things: how much money those companies earn, and how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of those earnings (a ratio called the price-to-earnings multiple, or P/E). This terminal tracks both parts separately, using what the market expected at each moment in history -- not just what actually happened later -- so that expectations can be compared against what was eventually delivered. Prices move when either piece changes: earnings can grow or shrink, and the multiple investors are willing to pay can expand or contract, often for reasons that have little to do with the earnings themselves. Keeping the two apart is the whole point of this tool.

The data

SeriesSourceSpanObservationsLast dateOne honest limitation

The views

Click any card to switch to that view.

Honest limits

Everything on this terminal labeled "expected" or "priced at each date" reflects what the market believed at the time -- not what turned out to be true; the Forecast accuracy view is the one place that scores the gap between the two. Data is captured roughly monthly (weekly in some stretches), so this terminal cannot see what happened between two snapshots -- a sharp move and reversal within a single month would be invisible here. Finally, because many of the return windows on this terminal overlap (a 2020 one-year return and a 2021 one-year return share nine months of the same market), the number of data points shown is not the number of independent observations -- a decade of monthly snapshots behaves more like a handful of truly separate market episodes, not dozens. One more limit worth naming: every expectation on this terminal is FactSet's bottom-up analyst consensus, not "the market" itself. Analysts revise their estimates with a lag, so at fast turning points (March 2020, 2022) the forward multiple partly reflects slow-to-update estimates rather than a pure read on investor sentiment.

Is the market expensive relative to its own history?
The gap between trailing and forward is the earnings growth already paid for.
Forward return attribution: Expected earnings or the multiple?
Forward mode explains whether the market move was supported by changing earnings expectations or multiple changes.
What are stocks paying over bonds?
Where does the S&P 500 land in about a year, for each combination of earnings delivery and exit multiple?
A snapshot from the latest data, not a time series -- range buttons and date pickers do not apply here.

Did earnings deliver what the market paid for?
This year's valuation path against every prior year.
Always shows full history by construction -- range buttons and date pickers do not apply here.

Valuation versus subsequent

Descriptive, not predictive: the fit is weak at the 1-year horizon (single-year returns are dominated by noise), and tightens with horizon as 3-year annualized returns average out short-term swings. Dashed vertical line = current forward 12-month P/E.
Returns here include dividends (); price-only figures are retained in the underlying data, just not plotted in this view. The drawdown ledger below remains price-only (conventional).
Scope: this fit is in-sample, over a single decade dominated by one interest-rate regime, and its points are overlapping 12-month windows rather than independent observations (see the Forecast accuracy note on effective sample size). No out-of-sample test exists yet.

Expected-return panel (5-year annualized)

g default 7% (long-run norm, deliberately not the current cyclical consensus) · d default % (from Shiller dividend data)

Drawdown ledger (peak-to-trough decline of 10% or more, newest first)

Other sources

Guide -- what every series is, its formula, its source, and how to read it
SeriesWhat it isFormulaSourceHow to read it
FactSet bottom-up adjusted (non-GAAP) EPS is the live basis for all series above except where labeled "S&P annex" (frozen, ends Dec 2025). The two bases are never mixed or spliced -- see the workbook ReadMe and PROJECT_HISTORY.md for the full never-mix rule and wedge quantification.
Render-only terminal. Data is captured weekly (Cowork/Claude Code); this file is rebuilt by scripts/build_terminal.py -- never hand-edited.
